summary: Patrick McKenzie outlines three tactical approaches for software businesses to capitalize on seasonal demand spikes: mini-sites optimized for holiday keywords, targeted PPC campaigns with seasonal landing pages, and strategic email marketing to existing trial users. Drawing from his experience selling bingo card software to teachers, he demonstrates how October/Halloween drives his best month annually. He emphasizes building reusable infrastructure for rapid campaign deployment and explains the powerful economics of trading digital IP for conversions. The post is a practical playbook showing that even niche software businesses have seasonal patterns worth exploiting.
key_points: Most software developers wrongly assume demand is static year-round; identifying and exploiting seasonal spikes can dramatically boost revenue
Mini-sites with holiday-specific domains and content can rank easily for seasonal keywords at low cost, serving as targeted funnels separate from your main site
Building reusable landing page infrastructure lets you launch seasonal campaigns in minutes rather than hours, making experimentation nearly free
Email marketing to existing trial users during seasonal peaks is highly efficient because they already trust your product and just need a timely nudge
Digital IP can be replicated at zero marginal cost, making it an extraordinarily efficient incentive to offer customers in exchange for moving toward conversion
money_quotes: Many software/web-app developers naively assume that demand for their product is roughly static year-round, despite the fact that this is true for very, very few industries.
If software developers can't respect license terms then we are doooooomed.
Not only can you do email marketing in a non-spammy fashion, some people actually enjoy it so much that they'll email you to ask why the September newsletter hasn't arrived yet.
Fear and distrust of the Internet and the evil virus-spreading Spammy McPhishertons on it runs rampant in my market, so I spend significant efforts trying to demonstrate that I'm on the up-and-up.

Any IP you create can be replicated essentially indefinitely for no marginal cost, much like your main product. However, customers don't see the world that way — they live in a world where scarcity is a reality, and accordingly perceive value in things they don't have but might want.
The economics of this are staggeringly efficient compared to e.g. PPC advertising, and you're giving the offer to folks who are already pre-disposed to liking you.
Both making the offer and fulfilling it can be automated, so it costs a static amount of labor no matter how many customers you have and how many take you up on the offer.
key_insight: Software businesses should identify their seasonal demand spikes and build reusable infrastructure for mini-sites, PPC campaigns, and email promotions that can be deployed rapidly at near-zero marginal cost.
